SAP HANA Cloud supports vector storage natively via the REAL_VECTOR data type, and cosine similarity via a built-in SQL function. The similarity computation stays inside the database engine. No rows are loaded into application memory, no separate vector service is needed:

SELECT TOP 5 ID, DESCRIPTION, FILTER_TEMPLATE, SERVICE_NAME,
  COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) AS SCORE
FROM ODATA_PATTERNS
ORDER BY SCORE DESC

The embedding model is text-embedding-3-small via SAP AI Core, at 1536 dimensions. At write time, each pattern's description is first expanded by an LLM into 8-15 synonym phrasings before embedding. "Purchase orders by supplier" becomes "POs for vendor, orders by BP, vendor PO list, supplier purchase orders..." All of that gets embedded together into a single vector, so the embedding represents the range of business phrasings a user might realistically use, not just the original description.

What improved: three behaviors in practice

In this prototype, the reranker is a second LLM check that reviews the top retrieved candidates and either confirms the best match or rejects the query as unsupported. Queries flow through three paths depending on the cosine score: fast path (score at or above 0.65, no LLM), LLM reranker (0.45 to 0.65), or auto-reject (below 0.45).

Vocabulary mismatch: where the improvement was clearest

The clearest improvement was on vocabulary mismatch. "Vendor invoices not yet paid" matched "Show supplier invoices in the system" with a cosine score of 0.516. Only one word overlaps: invoices. Full-text search would likely have treated this as a weak match. Vector search found the semantic proximity and, after LLM reranking confirmed the match, returned live invoice data from S/4HANA.

"Vendor invoices not yet paid" matched "Show supplier invoices in the system." Only "invoices" overlaps as a keyword. Vector search found the semantic proximity that keyword search misses.

The fast path: zero LLM calls for confident matches

When the cosine score is at or above 0.65, the system accepts the match and executes without any LLM call. In this test case, "Open sales orders" scored 0.679. The curated pattern encoded the relevant S/4HANA status filter: OverallSDProcessStatus eq 'A'. The query ran, and live sales order data came back in one round trip to HANA and one to S/4HANA.

Slot extraction: filling values from the question

When a pattern has {{placeholders}}, the LLM extracts values from the question. "Purchase orders by 17401710" - the vendor ID was detected, Supplier eq '17401710' composed, and PO 4500000060 returned live from S/4HANA.

"17401710" extracted as supplier_id. Filter composed. PO 4500000060 returned from S/4HANA.

Generating patterns from OData specs

A pattern store that starts empty is not useful. Writing patterns by hand is slow: 8 curated patterns cover 3 domains. A real SAP landscape spans dozens of APIs and hundreds of relevant queries.

The OData specification files (OpenAPI/Edmx JSON committed alongside the code) contain much of what an LLM needs to propose candidate patterns: entity sets, properties, filter value enumerations, relationships. An LLM running on SAP AI Core reads a spec and proposes patterns with filter templates. One run, seven specs, 139 candidate patterns, without hand-authoring.

Seven OData specs. 140 generated candidate patterns. Business Partner, Maintenance Order, Outbound Delivery, Purchase Order, Purchase Requisition, Sales Order, Supplier Invoice.

In my test cases, generated patterns worked for common queries. "Purchase requisitions waiting for approval" matched a generated pattern using ReleaseIsNotCompleted eq true on A_PurchaseRequisitionItem, the expected field in my test system.

A generated pattern using the expected SAP status field. Real requisition data returned.

Generated patterns enter the store as candidates: SOURCE='generated', VALIDATED=FALSE. They can be retrieved and executed, but they are not promoted until confirmed.

On trust: HTTP 200 alone is not sufficient to promote a pattern. A successful response with zero records, as the next section shows, looks the same as an HTTP 200 with valid results. Promotion requires result-shape checks, expected outcome checks, repeated successful usage, or explicit human review. Execution feedback accumulates trust over time; it does not grant it immediately.

Where it failed: the "SAP SE" case

Then came the more interesting failure: "Purchase orders by SAP SE."

The slot signal detector correctly identified "SAP SE" as a supplier name after a preposition. The LLM extracted supplier_id = "SAP SE". The OData call executed, HTTP 200, and returned zero records.

Structurally correct. Semantically useless. S/4HANA filters on vendor number 17401710, not the string "SAP SE." The execution completes with HTTP 200 and returns nothing. No error. No signal that anything went wrong.

This is the failure mode that most AI demonstrations avoid showing. There is no error, no stack trace. The system did exactly what it was designed to do and returned a response indistinguishable from "no matching purchase orders exist," when the real situation was that the query was structurally correct but semantically wrong.

This is not an edge case in SAP environments. Users think in supplier names, customer names, plant names, and material descriptions. The system identifiers (vendor number, plant code, material group) are database keys that users encounter only incidentally. For genuinely unsupported questions, the reranker returns a clean rejection; that part worked. The harder problem was when the system returned a technically successful but semantically empty result.

What this taught me about retrieval vs reasoning

Semantic retrieval solves matching. It does not solve interpretation. In SAP, users ask in business language; the APIs work in system identifiers. Closing that gap, reliably, at scale, requires something beyond embeddings.

Vector search genuinely improved vocabulary mismatch: "vendor invoices not yet paid" finding "Show supplier invoices," "Open" resolving through a curated status-code pattern. These hold up in practice.

What vector search does not address is the gap between business language and system identifiers. When a user says "SAP SE," the retrieval system correctly identifies the intent (supplier filter) and correctly identifies the slot (supplier_id). The embedding did its job. The failure is downstream: filling a system identifier slot with a human-readable name. No similarity threshold, no synonym expansion, no reranker fixes this. It requires a lookup, a small MDM problem embedded inside a retrieval problem.

What this implies about trust is equally important: a pattern can be structurally correct and semantically wrong at the same time. HTTP 200 is not a signal of correctness; it is a signal of execution. A zero-record result from a name-based filter is indistinguishable from a zero-record result from a legitimate empty query. The trust pipeline has to account for this, which means execution feedback alone is insufficient as a validation signal.

How patterns earn trust: the full lifecycle

  1. OData spec to LLM generator: reads entity sets and fields, proposes candidate patterns with filter templates
  2. Candidate pattern: enters store as SOURCE=generated, VALIDATED=FALSE
  3. Validation: field checks, allowlist checks, result-shape checks
  4. S/4HANA execution: result-shape and expected outcome checks; pass promotes the pattern
  5. VALIDATED=TRUE: execution count accumulates; repeated failure flags the pattern for review

Trust accumulates through use, not assumption. Promotion requires result-shape checks, repeated successful usage, or human review. HTTP 200 alone is not enough.

What comes next

Three open problems remain within the pattern store model, becoming more acute as the corpus grows.

Score calibration at scale. The 0.65 threshold was calibrated on 139 patterns where the right answer is almost always the top result. With 5,000 patterns across 50 domains, the embedding space becomes denser. Adjacent domain vocabulary competes, the reranker fires more often, and latency compounds. Domain pre-filtering before the cosine comparison (WHERE SERVICE_NAME = ?) is the structural fix, but requires knowing the domain before retrieval.

Slot values as system identifiers. "SAP SE" is the visible case, but the same gap exists for plant codes, material group codes, customer numbers, and cost center codes. Every slot that takes a master data value has this problem. Closing it requires a lookup layer between slot detection and slot filling.

Single-shot retrieval has a reasoning ceiling. The pattern store maps one question to one pattern. That covers a large share of transactional queries. But some questions require chaining: look up a supplier, then find their open POs; find the goods receipt for this invoice; show everything in this Procure-to-Pay process. These cannot be answered by retrieving a single pattern.

Part 3: retrieval based on API semantics

Part 2 improved retrieval inside a pattern-store model. Part 3 moves closer to the A2A goal: API-semantic retrieval, runtime tool loops, and multi-step SAP business capability execution.

Spoiler
Running alongside the pattern store, I explored a different retrieval model, one that embeds entire API specifications rather than individual query patterns, and uses an LLM tool loop to compose queries at runtime rather than filling pre-validated templates. That approach handled "purchase orders by SAP SE" more naturally, because the LLM could chain a supplier lookup before the purchase order query. But it surfaces different questions about trust, scale, and how you validate dynamically generated OData calls. Part 3 covers how that system works, where it scales, and where it does not.

This post reflects a personal learning exercise built on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry with HANA Cloud and SAP AI Core. All views are my own and do not represent SAP's positions, roadmap, or product direction. Code snippets are from personal prototypes and are shared for illustrative purposes only.

entry.title: Why Knowledge Transition Looks Complete but Still Fails After S/4HANA Go-Live
entry.content:
Why Knowledge Transition Looked Complete but Still Failed After Our S/4HANA Go-Live

Introduction

Over the years, I’ve been involved in many SAP AMS transitions, covering everything from S/4HANA and ECC to Basis operations and production support handovers. One thing I’ve noticed time and again is that knowledge transition (KT) often ends up feeling like just another box to tick.

Sessions get done, documents get uploaded, trackers turn green, and everyone feels ready to move into support. But in one of my S/4HANA projects, I learned the hard way that ticking all the KT boxes on paper doesn’t always mean the AMS team is truly prepared for the realities of production.

This experience changed how I now approach KT, especially for complex Finance environments with lots of interfaces.

The Project Situation

This project involved:

  • SAP S/4HANA implementation
  • FI integration with external accounting systems
  • Multiple inbound interfaces using IDocs
  • Batch job-heavy month-end processing
  • AMS takeover after hypercare

I was coordinating between the project team, Basis team, and AMS support teams to ensure support readiness. During KT, everything seemed very organised. The project team gave detailed walkthroughs covering:

  • FI posting flows
  • IDoc architecture
  • Background job schedules
  • Monitoring activities
  • Common error handling steps
  • Escalation contacts

The Basis team also explained standard monitoring transactions and daily health checks.

By the end of KT:

  • All KT trackers were marked complete
  • Sign-offs were obtained
  • Shadow support was completed
  • Readiness status was green

At that point, everyone felt confident.

What Happened After Go-Live

 The system was stable for the first few weeks after go-live. But the real challenge hit during the first month-end closing cycle.

I still remember one late-night support call when the finance team reported delays in accounting postings coming from external systems. At first, nothing seemed critical:

  • No dumps in ST22
  • No major system alerts
  • Background jobs were running
  • Interfaces were technically active

The AMS team’s first thought was, “The system looks fine. Let’s check with the project team.” But as we dug deeper, we noticed unusual queue behaviour:

  • During one month-end cycle, we noticed nearly 8,000 inbound FI IDocs sitting in status 64 for more than 20 minutes.


     

    Example of inbound FI IDocs waiting in status 64 during peak month-end processing.


  • SM58 showed growing tRFC entries

Example of queue buildup observed during high-volume month-end processing.

  • SMQ1 queues gradually building up
  • Some FI-related background jobs that normally finished within minutes were taking significantly longer during peak load.

The tricky part was there was no outright system failure. The issue crept in slowly under peak load. While the AMS team knew the monitoring tools and technical setup, they weren’t fully confident whether this was just normal temporary load or an early warning of a bottleneck. That uncertainty delayed troubleshooting and escalation decisions.

What We Realised Was Missing in KT

After things stabilised, we reviewed the KT approach and realised something important:

The KT sessions had focused heavily on system design and support steps, but not enough on how the system actually behaves in real production.

We covered:

  • Configuration walkthroughs
  • Interface structures
  • Monitoring steps
  • Job schedules
  • Known issue handling

But we hadn’t talked enough about:

  • System behaviour during peak month-end load
  • Queue buildup patterns seen in performance testing
  • Critical timing overlaps between interfaces and batch jobs
  • Temporary manual monitoring done during hypercare
  • Known “sensitive windows” during closing cycles

I remember a project team member saying, “We saw similar queue buildup during testing, but it cleared automatically, so we didn’t think it was a big risk.”

That stuck with me. If we had openly shared that during KT, the AMS team could have reacted much faster in production.

What We Changed in Later Projects

After that, we changed how we handle KT in future S/4HANA projects. Instead of just transferring system knowledge, we started focusing on operational realities.

During KT, we began asking questions like:

  • Where did the system struggle during testing?
  • Which interfaces are most sensitive during peak load?
  • Which batch jobs often overlap?
  • What temporary monitoring was needed during hypercare?
  • What warning signs should AMS teams watch for?

From the Basis side, these discussions became especially valuable. We started documenting:

  • Expected queue growth patterns
  • Critical SM58 monitoring scenarios
  • High-risk job execution windows
  • Typical month-end workload spikes
  • Situations where manual intervention was needed before

We also improved shadow support. Instead of immediately passing issues back to the project team, we encouraged AMS teams to try initial troubleshooting themselves, with project teams available in the background. It slowed things down at first but built much stronger support confidence after go-live.

Key Learning

The biggest lesson I learned is that knowledge transfer isn’t just about explaining the system. It’s about sharing real operational experience.

Documents can cover configurations, transactions, and monitoring steps, but true production readiness comes from understanding how the system behaves under pressure, during busy periods, and when things don’t go as planned.

In most projects, that knowledge stays with the project team unless someone makes a conscious effort to share it during KT.

Conclusion

In many SAP projects, KT is treated as a milestone to tick off before AMS takeover. But from what I’ve seen, successful KT depends less on how many documents are shared and more on honest conversations about real operational behaviour.

Sometimes the most valuable KT discussions aren’t about system architecture at all. They’re about questions like:

  • Where did we struggle during testing?
  • What nearly broke during hypercare?
  • Which processes make the team nervous at month-end?

Those conversations prepare AMS teams far better than any checklist can.

In my experience, KT truly succeeds only when teams share not just system knowledge, but also the operational judgement built through the project journey.

entry.title: T-Code : SOAMANAGER and OPD issue (screen blinking, no page opening)
entry.content:
T-Code : SOAMANAGER and OPD  issue (screen blinking, no page opening)

When I execute SOAMANAGER  or OPD T-Codes, screen blinks but page doesn't open.

Solution:  It is know issue with SAP

##0003729656 - Web transaction broken after Microsoft Edge update 146.0.3856.62

Workaround 1 –

Open below link in Edge browser and add SAP GUI connector -

SAP GUI connector for Microsoft Edge

https://microsoftedge.microsoft.com/addons/detail/sap-gui-connector-for-mic/mhpigfckgphoiifbehgajfbkocihbaho

Workaround 2 –

please follow the workaround as below –

It is very simple /nSBWP  from that screen /osoamanager

entry.title: Why data fragmentation limits AI in SAP landscapes
entry.content:
Almost every conversation these days related to SAP contains some reference to AI. This doesn't surprise me, as I don't know any person today that it's not acquainted with AI somehow. Even those whose knowledge on technology is very little have become accostumed to it.

When it comes to enterprise technology, AI has already proven capabilities, along with the validation of real-world implementations. Models being created today can predict outcomes, automate processes, and produce insights like never before — we personally have performed multiple trials in a controlled environment that provided us with very successful outcomes. However, the problems begin to occur when you step outside of these controlled environments and try to integrate AI into how businesses truly function, because generally speaking, there is an issue unrelated to the actual model. I think things would be easier if the following issue was understood: almost all problems that come with the use of AI, even in enterprise technology, are entirely human.

The most prevalent issue we meet is with the current state of the data on which those models are built. The SAP landscape has not been built in a straightforward, linear manner. SAP environments develop over time, sometimes decades, and they accumulate all types of complexity from different systems, customizations, and integrations. What you actually find when trying to integrate AI into a business is not one version of a “true” set of data; it’s numerous versions of that same set of data existing simultaneously. This is where the difficulty in the implementation of AI begins.

From our observations, typically when implemented in a targeted manner, AI can quickly demonstrate its intended utility. You understand what it is you want to do, prepare your data and create your model, then produce relevant results. That’s not the problem. The contingency occurs immediately after implementation when there is momentum to take that across the enterprise as part of normal operations. At the time of enterprise rollout, the model is no longer operating against a pre-qualified dataset, but is functioning against the full complexity of the SAP ecosystem. Data is sourced from multiple places, using different formats, at different frequencies and sometimes even with differing definitions of the same element. The model continues to perform in its intended manner, but at this point, it is performing on a fragmented foundation.

This scenario was demonstrated to us on a recent project in the energy market where our objective was to forecast energy demand and supply as well as price by aggregating historical production and operational data with meteorological variables. The end product was a neural network model that has been trained with 3+ years of historical data, and the results will be made available through a dashboard to provide users with expected outcomes in order to support their decision-making process.

When viewed through the lens of modeling, everything operated as expected. The model predictions aligned with the actual results and provided analytical evidence to support previously undiscovered trends. However, the challenge was not necessarily the model itself, but rather the data that it was being provided either directly or indirectly to derive these insights.

We aggregated a multitude of data sources including electricity data, weather patterns, and pricing data to make predictions and develop forecasts. Each data source had its own unique structure, timing and granularity. Therefore, there were occasions where small misalignment between these disparate data sources would result in inconsistent predicted results.

This is where the most significant issues began to emerge, as various teams were relying on a different set of datasets when the model generated a forecast, which led to a disconnect from one team to the next. As such, teams would validate the model output against their own internal generated data, which would then lead to the model output being challenged instead of acted upon. While the insights were available, confidence in the insights was not. We’ve seen this pattern countless times, where fragmented data doesn’t stop AI from generating insights, but it does stop people from trusting and acting on them.

What we've learned is that scaling AI in SAP environments is actually a lot more about fixing the underlying data than about building a better AI model. Once you get your data to be more consistent and integrated and shared more easily across systems, everything else becomes a lot easier to do and move along at a faster pace.

We have experienced firsthand how a technically safe solution can have trouble because of data alignment issues, and we have seen how fairly simple models can actually generate significant value as long as your data foundation is solid. So the difference between these two situations is not the AI itself, but rather the environment in which the AI is being used.

Ultimately, the only time AI will matter is when it can lead to actual business decisions that take place. And in SAP landscapes, the transition from insight to action is very much dependent upon the connectivity and reliability of the data.

Therefore, while it may seem logical to focus on what your model can do for you, the more critical question for businesses is whether their data is ready to support scaling of AI. If it's not, scaling AI will always feel harder than it should be, no matter how good your models are.

entry.title: SAP Enterprise Support Academy Newsletter May 2026
entry.content:
